[North East Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_East_Lincolnshire "North East Lincolnshire")

([/ˈlɪŋkənʃər, -ʃɪər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") and [Yorkshire and the Humber](/wiki/Yorkshire_and_the_Humber "Yorkshire and the Humber") regions of England. It is bordered by the [East Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/East_Riding_of_Yorkshire "East Riding of Yorkshire") across the [Humber](/wiki/Humber "Humber") estuary to the north, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the east, [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk"), [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire"), [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire") and [Rutland](/wiki/Rutland "Rutland") to the south, and [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire"), [Nottinghamshire](/wiki/Nottinghamshire "Nottinghamshire") and [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire") to the west.

**Lincolnshire** ([/ˈlɪŋkənʃər, -ʃɪər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") and [Yorkshire and the Humber](/wiki/Yorkshire_and_the_Humber "Yorkshire and the Humber") regions of England. It is bordered by the [East Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/East_Riding_of_Yorkshire "East Riding of Yorkshire") across the [Humber](/wiki/Humber "Humber") estuary to the north, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the east, [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk"), [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire"), [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire") and [Rutland](/wiki/Rutland "Rutland") to the south, and [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire"), [Nottinghamshire](/wiki/Nottinghamshire "Nottinghamshire") and [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire") to the west.

The county is predominantly rural, with an area of 6,959 km2 (2,687 sq mi) and an estimated population of 1,120,749 in 2024. The port town of [Grimsby](/wiki/Grimsby "Grimsby") is in the north-east coast of the county, [Boston](/wiki/Boston,_Lincolnshire "Boston, Lincolnshire") in the south-east, [Grantham](/wiki/Grantham "Grantham") in the south-west, the city of [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_England "Lincoln, England") in the west, and [Scunthorpe](/wiki/Scunthorpe "Scunthorpe") in the north-west.[[8]](#cite_note-8) For [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") purposes Lincolnshire comprises a [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county"), with seven districts, and the [unitary authority areas](/wiki/Unitary_authority_area "Unitary authority area") of [North Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_Lincolnshire "North Lincolnshire") and [North East Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_East_Lincolnshire "North East Lincolnshire"). The last two areas are part of the [Yorkshire and the Humber](/wiki/Yorkshire_and_the_Humber "Yorkshire and the Humber") region, and the rest of the county is in the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands"). The non-metropolitan county council and two unitary councils collaborate through the [Greater Lincolnshire Combined County Authority](/wiki/Greater_Lincolnshire_Combined_County_Authority "Greater Lincolnshire Combined County Authority").

The county is the second largest in England and has a varied geography. The south-east contains part of [the Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens"), a naturally marshy region which has been drained for agriculture, and the south-west is an upland region.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) A wide [vale](/wiki/Valley "Valley") runs north–south from the centre to the north of the county.[[11]](#cite_note-Lincolnshire_Wolds-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) To its east, the chalk hills of the [Lincolnshire Wolds](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Wolds "Lincolnshire Wolds"),[[11]](#cite_note-Lincolnshire_Wolds-11) which have been designated a [national landscape](/wiki/National_Landscape "National Landscape"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) occupy the north-east, with a coastal plain and the [Lincolnshire Marsh](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Marsh "Lincolnshire Marsh") beyond.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The west of the vale is demarcated by the [Lincolnshire Edge](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Edge "Lincolnshire Edge"), a long escarpment; at its northern end are the Coversands, an area of [heath](/wiki/Heath "Heath").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) Beyond the edge, the western border of the county contains the eastern part of the [Trent Valley](/wiki/Trent_Valley "Trent Valley") and, in the north, part of the [Humberhead Levels](/wiki/Humberhead_Levels "Humberhead Levels"),[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) with the [River Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent") itself forming part of the border.

Lincolnshire has had a comparatively quiet history, being a rural county which was not heavily industrialised and faced little threat of invasion. In the Roman era Lincoln was a major settlement, called [Lindum Colonia](/wiki/Lindum_Colonia "Lindum Colonia"). In the fifth century what would become the county was settled by the invading [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"), who established the [Kingdom of Lindsey](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Lindsey "Kingdom of Lindsey") in the north of the region. Lincoln became the centre of a [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") in 1072, and [Lincoln Cathedral](/wiki/Lincoln_Cathedral "Lincoln Cathedral") was built over the following centuries. The late Middle Ages were a particularly prosperous period, when wealth from [wool trade](/wiki/Medieval_English_wool_trade "Medieval English wool trade") facilitated the building of [grand churches](/wiki/Wool_church "Wool church") such as [St Botolph's Church, Boston](/wiki/St_Botolph%27s_Church,_Boston "St Botolph's Church, Boston"). During the Second World War the relatively flat topography of the county made it an important base for the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force"), which gave rise to the nickname 'Bomber County'.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Lincolnshire](/wiki/History_of_Lincolnshire "History of Lincolnshire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Lincolnia_nottinghamia_Atlas.jpg/250px-Lincolnia_nottinghamia_Atlas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincolnia_nottinghamia_Atlas.jpg)

Hand-drawn map of Lincolnshire and Nottinghamshire from 1576

During pre-Roman times, most of Lincolnshire was inhabited by the [Corieltauvi](/wiki/Corieltauvi "Corieltauvi") people.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The language of the area at that time would have been [Common Brittonic](/wiki/Common_Brittonic "Common Brittonic"), the precursor to modern [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"), [Cornish](/wiki/Cornish_language "Cornish language") and [Breton](/wiki/Breton_language "Breton language"). The name *Lincoln* was derived from *[Lindum Colonia](/wiki/Lindum_Colonia "Lindum Colonia")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Large numbers of Germanic speakers from continental Europe settled in the region following the withdrawal of the Romans. Though these were later identified as [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"), it is unlikely that they migrated as part of an organized tribal group.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Thus, the main language of the region quickly became [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English"). However, it is possible that Brittonic continued to be spoken in some communities as late as the eighth century.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Modern-day Lincolnshire is derived from the merging of the territory of the [Kingdom of Lindsey](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Lindsey "Kingdom of Lindsey") with that controlled by the [Danelaw](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") [borough](/wiki/Borough "Borough") of [Stamford](/wiki/Stamford,_Lincolnshire "Stamford, Lincolnshire"). For some time the entire county was called "Lindsey", and it is recorded as such in the 11th-century *[Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book").* Later, the name [Lindsey](/wiki/Lindsey_(government_district) "Lindsey (government district)") was applied to the northern core, around Lincoln. This emerged as one of the three [Parts of Lincolnshire](/wiki/Parts_of_Lincolnshire "Parts of Lincolnshire"), along with the Parts of [Holland](/wiki/Holland,_Lincolnshire "Holland, Lincolnshire") in the south-east, and the Parts of [Kesteven](/wiki/Kesteven "Kesteven") in the south-west, which each had separate [Quarter Sessions](/wiki/Quarter_Sessions "Quarter Sessions") as their county administrations. Lindsay was traditionally split between the [North](/wiki/North_Riding_of_Lindsey "North Riding of Lindsey"), [South](/wiki/South_Riding_of_Lindsey "South Riding of Lindsey") and [West Ridings of Lindsey](/wiki/West_Riding_of_Lindsey "West Riding of Lindsey").

The area was shaken by 27 February [2008 Lincolnshire earthquake](/wiki/2008_Lincolnshire_earthquake "2008 Lincolnshire earthquake"), reaching between 4.7 and 5.3 on the [Richter magnitude scale](/wiki/Richter_magnitude_scale "Richter magnitude scale"); it was one of the largest earthquakes to affect Britain in recent years.

Lincolnshire is home to [Woolsthorpe Manor](/wiki/Woolsthorpe_Manor "Woolsthorpe Manor"), birthplace and home of Sir [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton"). He attended [The King's School, Grantham](/wiki/The_King%27s_School,_Grantham "The King's School, Grantham"). Its library has preserved his signature, carved into a window sill when he was a youth.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Lincolnshire1832_Map.png/250px-Lincolnshire1832_Map.png)](/wiki/File:Lincolnshire1832_Map.png)

The historic county boundaries of Lincolnshire in 1832

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Lincolnshire_Admin_Counties_1890-1965.png/250px-Lincolnshire_Admin_Counties_1890-1965.png)](/wiki/File:Lincolnshire_Admin_Counties_1890-1965.png)

County and County Borough areas pre 1965

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Sutton_Washway.jpg/330px-Sutton_Washway.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sutton_Washway.jpg)

Until the early 19th Century there was no fixed land border between Lincolnshire and Norfolk as the two counties were separated by the former “Cross Keys Wash” which could only be crossed at low tide by a causeway.

The geographical layout of Lincolnshire is quite extensive and mostly separated by many rivers and rolling countryside. The north of the county begins from where the [Isle of Axholme](/wiki/Isle_of_Axholme "Isle of Axholme") is located near the meeting points of the rivers [Ouse](/wiki/River_Great_Ouse "River Great Ouse") and [Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent") near to the [Humber](/wiki/River_Humber "River Humber"). From there, the southside of the [Humber](/wiki/Humber "Humber") estuary forms the border between Lincolnshire and the [East Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/East_Riding_of_Yorkshire "East Riding of Yorkshire"). From there, the south bank of the Humber Estuary where the [Humber Bridge](/wiki/Humber_Bridge "Humber Bridge") crosses the estuary at [Barton upon Humber](/wiki/Barton_upon_Humber "Barton upon Humber"), is used primarily for the [shipping ports](/wiki/Shipping_port "Shipping port") at [Immingham](/wiki/Immingham "Immingham"), [New Holland](/wiki/New_Holland,_Lincolnshire "New Holland, Lincolnshire") and [Grimsby](/wiki/Grimsby "Grimsby"). From there, the rest of the southern bank forms the [Lincolnshire Coast](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Coast "Lincolnshire Coast") from [Cleethorpes](/wiki/Cleethorpes "Cleethorpes") to [Mablethorpe](/wiki/Mablethorpe "Mablethorpe") and then onto [Skegness](/wiki/Skegness "Skegness"). From Skegness, the rest of the Lincolnshire Coastline forms the sea boundary and border with [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") at the [Wash](/wiki/The_Wash "The Wash"). The coast then at [Boston](/wiki/Boston,_Lincolnshire "Boston, Lincolnshire") becomes the meeting point of the rivers [Welland](/wiki/River_Welland "River Welland") and [Haven](/wiki/River_Haven "River Haven") in an area known as the "Fosdyke Wash".

The rest of the sea boundary runs from [Fosdyke](/wiki/Fosdyke "Fosdyke") to the east of [Sutton Bridge](/wiki/Sutton_Bridge "Sutton Bridge"), where the current land boundary with [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") is located in a narrow area of reclaimed farmland just to the east of the [River Nene](/wiki/River_Nene "River Nene") but until as recently as the early 19th century there was no land border between Lincolnshire and Norfolk as it was separated from each other by the "Cross Keys Wash" a former area of estuary and marshland where the River Nene used to flow out into the Wash and could only be crossed at low tide by a causeway or ferry and was the natural boundary between the two counties. The causeway known at the time as the "Wash Way" was renowned as being particularly treacherous and the safer route was to go into Norfolk from Lincolnshire via the Cambridgeshire town of [Wisbech](/wiki/Wisbech "Wisbech") and this element remains to the present day as the [Cross Keys Bridge](/wiki/Cross_Keys_Bridge "Cross Keys Bridge") at [Sutton Bridge](/wiki/Sutton_Bridge "Sutton Bridge") provides the only direct access point to Norfolk from Lincolnshire over the [River Nene](/wiki/River_Nene "River Nene") some nine miles north of Wisbech. The border with Lincolnshire to [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire") begins at [Crowland](/wiki/Crowland "Crowland"), [Market Deeping](/wiki/Market_Deeping "Market Deeping") and [Stamford](/wiki/Stamford,_Lincolnshire "Stamford, Lincolnshire") which form the southern boundary of the county with both [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough "Peterborough"), [Rutland](/wiki/Rutland "Rutland") and briefly [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire"); the county's border with [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire") is just 20 yards (19 m) long, England's shortest county boundary.[[22]](#cite_note-22) From there, the border with [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire") and [Nottinghamshire](/wiki/Nottinghamshire "Nottinghamshire") begins at [Sleaford](/wiki/Sleaford "Sleaford"), [Grantham](/wiki/Grantham "Grantham"), [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_Lincolnshire "Lincoln, Lincolnshire") and [Gainsborough](/wiki/Gainsborough,_Lincolnshire "Gainsborough, Lincolnshire"). From Gainsborough, the border with [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire") begins at [Haxey](/wiki/Haxey "Haxey") and [Epworth](/wiki/Epworth,_Lincolnshire "Epworth, Lincolnshire") before looping back to the original north of the county near [Scunthorpe](/wiki/Scunthorpe "Scunthorpe") with East Riding of Yorkshire at the Isle of Axholme and [Goole](/wiki/Goole "Goole").[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Bedrock in Lincolnshire features Jurassic [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") (near Lincoln) and Cretaceous [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk") (north-east). The area around [Woodhall Spa](/wiki/Woodhall_Spa "Woodhall Spa") and [Kirkby on Bain](/wiki/Kirkby_on_Bain "Kirkby on Bain") is dominated by gravel and sand.[[34]](#cite_note-34) For much of prehistory, Lincolnshire was under tropical seas, and most fossils found in the county are marine invertebrates. Marine vertebrates have also been found including [ichthyosaurus](/wiki/Ichthyosaurus "Ichthyosaurus") and [plesiosaur](/wiki/Plesiosaur "Plesiosaur").[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The highest point in Lincolnshire is [Wolds Top](/wiki/Wolds_Top "Wolds Top") (168 m, 551 ft), at [Normanby le Wold](/wiki/Normanby_le_Wold "Normanby le Wold").[[37]](#cite_note-37) Some parts of [the Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens") may be below sea level. The nearest mountains are in Derbyshire.

The biggest rivers in Lincolnshire are the [Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent"), running northwards from [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") up the western edge of the county to the [Humber](/wiki/Humber "Humber") estuary, and the [Witham](/wiki/River_Witham "River Witham"), which begins in Lincolnshire at [South Witham](/wiki/South_Witham "South Witham") and runs for 132 km (82 miles) through the middle of the county, eventually emptying into the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") at [The Wash](/wiki/The_Wash "The Wash"). The Humber estuary, on Lincolnshire's northern border, is also fed by the [River Ouse](/wiki/River_Ouse,_Yorkshire "River Ouse, Yorkshire"). The Wash is also the mouth of the [Welland](/wiki/River_Welland "River Welland"), the [Nene](/wiki/River_Nene "River Nene") and the [Great Ouse](/wiki/Great_Ouse "Great Ouse").

Lincolnshire's geography is fairly varied, but consists of several distinct areas:

* [Lincolnshire Wolds](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Wolds "Lincolnshire Wolds"): area of rolling hills in the north-east of the county designated an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty
* [The Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens"): dominating the south-east quarter of the county
* [The Marshes](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Marsh "Lincolnshire Marsh"): running along the coast of the county
* [Lincoln Edge or Cliff](/wiki/Lincoln_Cliff "Lincoln Cliff"): limestone escarpment running north–south along the western half of the county

Lincolnshire's most well-known nature reserves include [Gibraltar Point](/wiki/Gibraltar_Point,_Lincolnshire "Gibraltar Point, Lincolnshire") National Nature Reserve, [Whisby Nature Park](/wiki/Whisby_Moor#Whisby_Nature_Park "Whisby Moor") Local Nature Reserve, [Donna Nook](/wiki/Donna_Nook "Donna Nook") National Nature Reserve, [RSPB Frampton Marsh](/wiki/RSPB_Frampton_Marsh "RSPB Frampton Marsh") and the [Humberhead Peatlands National Nature Reserve](/wiki/Humberhead_Levels "Humberhead Levels"). Although the Lincolnshire countryside is intensively farmed, there are many biodiverse wetland areas[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], as well as rare [limewood forests](/wiki/Bardney_Limewoods "Bardney Limewoods"). Much of the county was once wet fenland (see [The Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens")).

From bones, we can tell that animal species formerly found in Lincolnshire include [woolly mammoth](/wiki/Woolly_mammoth "Woolly mammoth"), [woolly rhinoceros](/wiki/Woolly_rhinoceros "Woolly rhinoceros"), [wild horse](/wiki/Tarpan "Tarpan"), [wolf](/wiki/Wolf "Wolf"), [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar") and [beaver](/wiki/Beaver "Beaver").[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) Species which have recently returned to Lincolnshire after [extirpation](/wiki/Extirpation "Extirpation") include [little egret](/wiki/Little_egret "Little egret"), [Eurasian spoonbill](/wiki/Eurasian_spoonbill "Eurasian spoonbill"), [European otter](/wiki/European_otter "European otter") and [red kite](/wiki/Red_kite "Red kite").[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Local government history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Local government history")]

The [Local Government Act 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888") established [county councils](/wiki/County_council "County council") for each of the parts of Lincolnshire – Lindsey, Holland and Kesteven – and came into effect on 1 April 1889. Lincoln was made an independent [county borough](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") on the same date, with [Grimsby](/wiki/Grimsby "Grimsby") following in 1891.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972") abolished the three county councils and the two county boroughs, effective 1 April 1974. On this date, Grimsby and the northern part of Lindsey (including [Scunthorpe](/wiki/Scunthorpe "Scunthorpe")) were amalgamated with most of the [East Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/East_Riding_of_Yorkshire "East Riding of Yorkshire") and a part of the [West Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/West_Riding_of_Yorkshire "West Riding of Yorkshire") to form the new [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") of [Humberside](/wiki/Humberside "Humberside"). The rest of Lindsey, along with Holland, Kesteven and Lincoln, came under the governance of the new [Lincolnshire County Council](/wiki/Lincolnshire_County_Council "Lincolnshire County Council").

A local government reform in 1996 abolished Humberside. The land south of the Humber Estuary was allocated to the [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authorities "Unitary authorities") of [North Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_Lincolnshire "North Lincolnshire") and [North East Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_East_Lincolnshire "North East Lincolnshire") which became part of Lincolnshire for ceremonial purposes, such as the [Lord-Lieutenancy](/wiki/Lord-Lieutenant "Lord-Lieutenant"), but are not covered by the Lincolnshire police; they are in the [Yorkshire and the Humber](/wiki/Yorkshire_and_the_Humber "Yorkshire and the Humber") region.
The remaining districts of Lincolnshire are [Boston](/wiki/Borough_of_Boston "Borough of Boston"), [East Lindsey](/wiki/East_Lindsey "East Lindsey"), Lincoln, [North Kesteven](/wiki/North_Kesteven "North Kesteven"), [South Holland](/wiki/South_Holland_District "South Holland District"), [South Kesteven](/wiki/South_Kesteven "South Kesteven"), and [West Lindsey](/wiki/West_Lindsey "West Lindsey"). They are part of the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") region.

[North East Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_East_Lincolnshire "North East Lincolnshire") and [North Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_Lincolnshire "North Lincolnshire") are unitary authorities. They were districts of [Humberside](/wiki/Humberside "Humberside") county from 1974.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In 1996, Humberside was abolished along with its county council.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Some services in those districts are shared with the [East Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/East_Riding_of_Yorkshire "East Riding of Yorkshire") ceremonial county, rather than the rest of Lincolnshire including [Humberside Police](/wiki/Humberside_Police "Humberside Police"), [Humberside Airport](/wiki/Humberside_Airport "Humberside Airport"), [Humberside Fire Service](/wiki/Humberside_Fire_Service "Humberside Fire Service"), and [BBC Radio Humberside](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Humberside "BBC Radio Humberside").

### Current governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Current governance")]

Since the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election") and the [constituency](/wiki/Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") reorganisation by the [2023 Periodic Review](/wiki/2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 review of Westminster constituencies"), Lincolnshire is represented by ten [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MPs) whose constituencies fall entirely within the county. Small areas of Lincolnshire form constituencies with parts of neighbouring counties, namely the [Isle of Axholme](/wiki/Isle_of_Axholme "Isle of Axholme") (part of [Doncaster East and the Isle of Axholme](/wiki/Doncaster_East_and_the_Isle_of_Axholme "Doncaster East and the Isle of Axholme")) and the town of [Stamford](/wiki/Stamford,_Lincolnshire "Stamford, Lincolnshire") and its surroundings (part of [Rutland and Stamford](/wiki/Rutland_and_Stamford "Rutland and Stamford")). Of the ten constituencies entirely within Lincolnshire, six are represented by the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), three by the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") and one by [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK").

[Lincolnshire County Council](/wiki/Lincolnshire_County_Council "Lincolnshire County Council") is majority controlled by Reform UK and consists of forty-four Reform UK councillors, fourteen Conservative, five [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"), three Labour, three [independents](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician"), and one [Lincolnshire Independent](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Independents "Lincolnshire Independents").[[45]](#cite_note-45) The county is made up of seven local borough and district councils and two unitary authority areas independent of the county council. The [City of Lincoln Council](/wiki/City_of_Lincoln_Council "City of Lincoln Council") is Labour-controlled. [North Kesteven](/wiki/North_Kesteven "North Kesteven"), [South Holland](/wiki/South_Holland_District "South Holland District") and [East Lindsey](/wiki/East_Lindsey "East Lindsey") are administered by the Conservatives. [South Kesteven](/wiki/South_Kesteven "South Kesteven") is controlled by a coalition of independent, Labour Party, Green Party and Liberal Democrat councillors. [West Lindsey](/wiki/West_Lindsey "West Lindsey") is controlled by a coalition of Liberal Democrats and independents. The [Borough of Boston](/wiki/Borough_of_Boston "Borough of Boston") is controlled by the local Boston Independent party. The unitary authority [North Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_Lincolnshire_Council "North Lincolnshire Council") and [North East Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_East_Lincolnshire_Council "North East Lincolnshire Council") councils are administered by the Conservative Party.

A mayoral devolution deal has received approval from the [Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Housing,_Communities_and_Local_Government "Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government"). This led to the establishment of a [Greater Lincolnshire Combined County Authority](/wiki/Greater_Lincolnshire_Combined_County_Authority "Greater Lincolnshire Combined County Authority"), formed of the county council, the two unitary authorities and the district councils, with powers over housing, job creation and public transport, including [bus franchising](/wiki/Bus_franchising "Bus franchising").[[46]](#cite_note-46) The first election for the [Mayor of Greater Lincolnshire](/wiki/Mayor_of_Greater_Lincolnshire "Mayor of Greater Lincolnshire"), who will chair GLCCA, took place in [May 2025](/wiki/2025_Greater_Lincolnshire_mayoral_election "2025 Greater Lincolnshire mayoral election").[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) Reform UK [Andrea Jenkyns](/wiki/Andrea_Jenkyns "Andrea Jenkyns") was elected and Reform UK took control of [Lincolnshire County Council](/wiki/Lincolnshire_County_Council "Lincolnshire County Council").[[49]](#cite_note-49)

#### Central Lincolnshire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Central Lincolnshire")]

The Central Lincolnshire area is a joint partnership arrangement between [North Kesteven](/wiki/North_Kesteven "North Kesteven"), [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_England "Lincoln, England") and [West Lindsey](/wiki/West_Lindsey "West Lindsey"), covering economic planning and development across the three districts.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Proposed local government reorganisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Proposed local government reorganisation")]

See also: [2024–present structural changes to local government in England](/wiki/2024%E2%80%93present_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England "2024–present structural changes to local government in England")

Separate to the newly established [devolution deal for Greater Lincolnshire](/wiki/Greater_Lincolnshire_Combined_County_Authority "Greater Lincolnshire Combined County Authority") three separate proposals for the future governance of Lincolnshire were published in March 2025. Option One would see a single council for the area that currently makes up Lincolnshire County Council and a merger of the current two unitary authorities of North Lincolnshire and North East Lincolnshire in the north of the county to make a single council. Option Two would see Lincolnshire split into two separate unitary authorities on a north/south basis with North Lincolnshire, North East Lincolnshire, East and West Lindsey making up the northern council area whilst Lincoln, North and South Kesteven, Boston and South Holland making up the southern council area. Option Three would see Lincolnshire split up into three council areas, one covering Lincoln, West Lindsey, North Kesteven and South Kesteven, the second covering East Lindsey, Boston and South Holland and the third covering North Lincolnshire and North East Lincolnshire.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Lincsfarmland.jpg/250px-Lincsfarmland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincsfarmland.jpg)

Lincolnshire farmland near [Burton Coggles](/wiki/Burton_Coggles "Burton Coggles")

Lincolnshire has long been a primarily agricultural area, and it continues to grow large amounts of [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"), [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley"), [sugar beet](/wiki/Sugar_beet "Sugar beet"), and [oilseed rape](/wiki/Oilseed_rape "Oilseed rape"). In south Lincolnshire, where the soil is particularly rich in nutrients, some of the most common crops include [potatoes](/wiki/Potatoes "Potatoes"), [cabbages](/wiki/Cabbage "Cabbage"), [cauliflowers](/wiki/Cauliflower "Cauliflower"), and [onions](/wiki/Onion "Onion"). Lincolnshire farmers often break world records for crop yields.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) South Lincolnshire is also home to one of the UK's leading [agricultural experiment stations](/wiki/Agricultural_experiment_station "Agricultural experiment station"), located in [Sutton Bridge](/wiki/Sutton_Bridge "Sutton Bridge") and operated by the [Potato Council](/wiki/Potato_Council "Potato Council"); Sutton Bridge Crop Storage Research engages in research for the British potato industry.[[57]](#cite_note-PCSB-57)

The [Lincoln Longwool](/wiki/Lincoln_sheep "Lincoln sheep") is a rare breed of sheep, named after the region, which was developed both for wool and mutton, at least 500 years ago, and has the longest fleece of any sheep breed.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The [Lincoln Red](/wiki/Lincoln_Red "Lincoln Red") is an old breed of beef cattle, originating from the county. In the mid 20th century most farms in Lincolnshire moved away from mixed farming to specialise in arable cropping, partly due to cheap wool imports, partly to take advantage of efficiencies of scale and partly because the drier land on the eastern side of England is particularly suitable for arable cropping.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Mechanisation around 1900 greatly diminished the number of workers required to operate the county's relatively large farms, and the proportion of workers in the agricultural sector dropped substantially during this period.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Several major engineering companies developed in Lincoln, [Gainsborough](/wiki/Gainsborough,_Lincolnshire "Gainsborough, Lincolnshire") and [Grantham](/wiki/Grantham "Grantham") to support those changes. Among these was [Fosters of Lincoln](/wiki/William_Foster_%26_Co_Ltd "William Foster & Co Ltd"), which built the first [tank](/wiki/Tank "Tank"), and [Richard Hornsby & Sons](/wiki/Richard_Hornsby_%26_Sons "Richard Hornsby & Sons") of Grantham. Most such industrial companies left during late 20th-century restructuring.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Today, [immigrant](/wiki/Immigration_to_Europe "Immigration to Europe") workers, mainly from [new member states of the European Union](/wiki/Enlargement_of_the_European_Union "Enlargement of the European Union") in Central and Eastern Europe, form a large component of the seasonal agricultural workforce, particularly in the south of the county. Here more labour-intensive crops are produced, such as small vegetables and cut flowers. This seasonal influx of migrant labour occasionally causes tension between the migrant workforce and local people, in a county which had been relatively unaccustomed to large-scale immigration.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Agricultural training is provided at [Riseholme College](/wiki/Riseholme_College "Riseholme College") and in 2016 the [University of Lincoln](/wiki/University_of_Lincoln "University of Lincoln") opened the Lincoln Institute for Agri-Food Technology.

## Public services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Public services")]

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Lincolnshire](/wiki/Education_in_Lincolnshire "Education in Lincolnshire")

Lincolnshire is one of the few counties in the UK that still uses the [eleven-plus](/wiki/Eleven-plus "Eleven-plus") to decide who may attend [grammar school](/wiki/Grammar_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "Grammar schools in the United Kingdom"). As a result, many towns in Lincolnshire have both a grammar school and a [secondary modern school](/wiki/Secondary_modern_school "Secondary modern school"). Lincolnshire's rural character means that some larger villages also have primary schools and are served by buses to nearby high schools.

Lincoln itself, however, is primarily non-selective, as is the area within a radius of about seven miles. In this area, almost all children attend [comprehensive schools](/wiki/Comprehensive_school "Comprehensive school"), though it is still possible to opt into the eleven-plus system. This gives rise to the unusual result that those who pass the eleven-plus can attend a grammar school outside the Lincoln comprehensive area, but those who do not pass still attend a (partly non-selective) comprehensive school.

Lincolnshire is also home of [University of Lincoln](/wiki/University_of_Lincoln "University of Lincoln").

### Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Health care")]

The [United Lincolnshire Hospitals NHS Trust](/wiki/United_Lincolnshire_Hospitals_NHS_Trust "United Lincolnshire Hospitals NHS Trust")[[60]](#cite_note-60) is one of the largest trusts in the country, employing almost 4,000 staff and with an annual budget of over £200 million. The north of the county is served by the [Northern Lincolnshire and Goole Hospital NHS Foundation Trust](http://www.nlg.nhs.uk/).

Some of the larger hospitals in the county include:

* [Diana Princess of Wales](/wiki/Diana_Princess_of_Wales "Diana Princess of Wales") Hospital, Grimsby
* [Scunthorpe General Hospital](/wiki/Scunthorpe_General_Hospital "Scunthorpe General Hospital")
* [Boston Pilgrim Hospital](/wiki/Pilgrim_Hospital "Pilgrim Hospital")
* [Lincoln County Hospital](/wiki/Lincoln_County_Hospital "Lincoln County Hospital")

Since April 1994, Lincolnshire has had an [air ambulance service](/wiki/Air_ambulances_in_the_United_Kingdom "Air ambulances in the United Kingdom").[[61]](#cite_note-61) The air ambulance is stationed at [RAF Waddington](/wiki/RAF_Waddington "RAF Waddington") near Lincoln and can reach emergencies in Lincolnshire within 25 minutes. An A&E hospital is only 10 minutes away by helicopter from any accident in Lincolnshire.

### Drainage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Drainage")]

Separately to the commercial water companies the low-lying parts of the county are drained by various [internal drainage boards](/wiki/Internal_drainage_board "Internal drainage board"), such as the [Black Sluice Internal Drainage Board](http://www.blacksluiceidb.gov.uk/), [Witham 4th District IDB](http://w4idb.co.uk/), [Lindsey Marsh Drainage Board](http://www.lmdb.co.uk/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20090418202643/http://www.lmdb.co.uk/) 18 April 2009 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"),[[62]](#cite_note-62) or the [Welland and Deepings Internal Drainage Board](http://www.wellandidb.org.uk/).[[63]](#cite_note-63)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in Lincolnshire](/wiki/Transport_in_Lincolnshire "Transport in Lincolnshire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Humber_Bridge_Sunset_Wideshot.jpg/250px-Humber_Bridge_Sunset_Wideshot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Humber_Bridge_Sunset_Wideshot.jpg)

The [Humber Bridge](/wiki/Humber_Bridge "Humber Bridge") connecting North Lincolnshire to the [East Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/East_Riding_of_Yorkshire "East Riding of Yorkshire")

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Roads")]

Being on the economic periphery of England, Lincolnshire's transport links are poorly developed compared with many other parts of the United Kingdom. The road network in the county is dominated by [single carriageway](/wiki/Single_carriageway "Single carriageway") [A roads](/wiki/Great_Britain_road_numbering_scheme "Great Britain road numbering scheme") and local roads (B roads) as opposed to [motorways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") and [dual carriageways](/wiki/Dual_carriageways "Dual carriageways"). The administrative county of Lincolnshire is one of the few UK counties without a motorway, and until several years ago, it was said that there was only about 35 km (22 mi) of dual carriageway in the whole of Lincolnshire. However the [M180 motorway](/wiki/M180_motorway "M180 motorway") passes through [North Lincolnshire](/wiki/North_Lincolnshire "North Lincolnshire"), splitting into two dual carriageway trunk roads to the [Humber Bridge](/wiki/Humber_Bridge "Humber Bridge") and [Grimsby](/wiki/Grimsby "Grimsby"), and the [A46](/wiki/A46_road "A46 road") is now dual carriageway between [Newark-on-Trent](/wiki/Newark-on-Trent "Newark-on-Trent") and Lincoln.

### Railways: history and present day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Railways: history and present day")]

The low population density of the county means there are few railway stations and train services, considering the county's large area. Many of the county's railway stations were permanently closed following the [Beeching Report](/wiki/Beeching_cuts "Beeching cuts") of 1963. The most notable reopening has been the line and two stations between Lincoln and [Sleaford](/wiki/Sleaford "Sleaford"), which reopened within months of the Beeching closure. Most other closed lines in the county were lifted long ago and much of the trackbed has returned to agricultural use.

Prior to 1970, a through train service operated between [Cleethorpes](/wiki/Cleethorpes_railway_station "Cleethorpes railway station") and [London King's Cross](/wiki/London_King%27s_Cross_railway_station "London King's Cross railway station") via [Louth](/wiki/Louth_railway_station "Louth railway station"), [Boston](/wiki/Boston_railway_station "Boston railway station") and [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough_railway_station "Peterborough railway station"). The part of this line in Grimsby is now the [A16 road](/wiki/A16_road_(England) "A16 road (England)"), preventing reinstatement as a railway line, and a small section of the line is now the [Lincolnshire Wolds Railway](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Wolds_Railway "Lincolnshire Wolds Railway"), with an extension towards Louth in progress.

A daily through train service operated between Cleethorpes and London King's Cross via [Grimsby](/wiki/Grimsby_railway_station "Grimsby railway station"), [Market Rasen](/wiki/Market_Rasen_railway_station "Market Rasen railway station") and [Lincoln Central](/wiki/Lincoln_railway_station "Lincoln railway station") until the late 1980s. The *Humberlincs Executive*, as the service was known, was operated by an [InterCity 125](/wiki/InterCity_125 "InterCity 125"), but was discontinued following the electrification of the [East Coast Main Line](/wiki/East_Coast_Main_Line "East Coast Main Line"). Passengers to/from London now have to change trains at [Newark North Gate](/wiki/Newark_North_Gate_railway_station "Newark North Gate railway station"). However, the East Coast Main Line passes through the western edge of the county and one can catch direct trains to London from [Grantham](/wiki/Grantham_railway_station "Grantham railway station").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Lincsroad.jpg/250px-Lincsroad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincsroad.jpg)

A rural road in Lincolnshire

Most rail services are currently operated by [East Midlands Railway](/wiki/East_Midlands_Railway "East Midlands Railway") and [Northern Trains](/wiki/Northern_Trains "Northern Trains"). [London North Eastern Railway](/wiki/London_North_Eastern_Railway "London North Eastern Railway") (LNER), [Hull Trains](/wiki/Hull_Trains "Hull Trains") and [CrossCountry](/wiki/CrossCountry "CrossCountry") have services which pass through the county, with LNER trains frequently passing and stopping at Grantham, on the East Coast Main Line and a service every other hour to [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln_railway_station "Lincoln railway station"), while CrossCountry trains stop at Stamford on their way between [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham_New_Street_railway_station "Birmingham New Street railway station") and [Stansted Airport](/wiki/Stansted_Airport_railway_station "Stansted Airport railway station"). Stations along the Humber are served by [TransPennine Express](/wiki/TransPennine_Express "TransPennine Express") services between [Manchester Airport](/wiki/Manchester_Airport_railway_station "Manchester Airport railway station") and Cleethorpes. One of the most infrequent services in the UK is in Lincolnshire: the [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield_railway_station "Sheffield railway station")-[Gainsborough Central](/wiki/Gainsborough_Central_railway_station "Gainsborough Central railway station")-Cleethorpes line has passenger trains only on a Saturday, with three trains in both directions. This line is, however, used for freight. Hull Trains also stops at Grantham before continuing its journey to either Kings Cross or Hull.

On 22 May 2011, [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_(train_operating_company) "East Coast (train operating company)") started a Lincoln-London service, initially one train a day each way, and there is a northbound service on a Sunday. This was increased in 2019 to a service every two hours. East Midlands Railway also run a daily (Mon-Sat) service each way between Lincoln and [London St Pancras](/wiki/St_Pancras_railway_station "St Pancras railway station"), though this is a stopping service which takes around three hours via [Nottingham](/wiki/Nottingham_railway_station "Nottingham railway station"), compared to LNER's service to London King's Cross which takes around 1 hour 50 minutes.

### Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Airport")]

The only airport in Lincolnshire is [Humberside Airport](/wiki/Humberside_Airport "Humberside Airport"), near Brigg. [East Midlands Airport](/wiki/East_Midlands_Airport "East Midlands Airport"), the main airport servicing the East Midlands, is within travelling distance[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] of the county. Until its closure in 2022, [Doncaster Sheffield Airport](/wiki/Doncaster_Sheffield_Airport "Doncaster Sheffield Airport") near [Doncaster](/wiki/Doncaster "Doncaster") was within travelling distance of much of Lincolnshire.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Buses")]

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Tourism")]

The majority of tourism in Lincolnshire relies on the coastal resorts and towns to the east of the [Lincolnshire Wolds](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Wolds "Lincolnshire Wolds"). The county has some of the best-known seaside resorts in the United Kingdom, which are a major attraction to visitors from across England, especially the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") and parts of [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire"). There are three main coastal resorts in Lincolnshire and several smaller village resorts:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Clock_Tower%2C_Skegness_1.JPG/250px-Clock_Tower%2C_Skegness_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Clock_Tower,_Skegness_1.JPG)

[Skegness](/wiki/Skegness "Skegness") town centre, showing the clock tower and the "Jolly Fisherman" sculpture/fountain

* The main county seaside resort of [Skegness](/wiki/Skegness "Skegness") with its famous [Jolly Fisherman](/wiki/The_Jolly_Fisherman "The Jolly Fisherman") mascot and famous slogan "Skegness is so bracing", together with its neighbouring large village coastal resorts of [Ingoldmells](/wiki/Ingoldmells "Ingoldmells") and [Chapel St Leonards](/wiki/Chapel_St_Leonards "Chapel St Leonards"), provides the biggest concentration of resorts along the [Lincolnshire Coast](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Coast "Lincolnshire Coast"), with many large caravan and holiday sites. The resort offers many amusements, beaches, leisure activities and shops, as well as [Butlins Skegness](/wiki/Butlins_Skegness "Butlins Skegness"), [Fantasy Island](/wiki/Fantasy_Island_(UK_amusement_park) "Fantasy Island (UK amusement park)"), [Church Farm Museum](/wiki/Church_Farm_Museum "Church Farm Museum"), [Natureland Seal Sanctuary](/wiki/Natureland_Seal_Sanctuary "Natureland Seal Sanctuary"), [Skegness Stadium](/wiki/Skegness_Stadium "Skegness Stadium"), [Skegness Pier](/wiki/Skegness_Pier#Pier "Skegness Pier") and several well-known local golf courses. There are good road, bus and rail links to the rest of the county.
* The second largest group of resorts along the coast is composed of the seaside towns of [Mablethorpe](/wiki/Mablethorpe "Mablethorpe") and [Sutton-on-Sea](/wiki/Sutton-on-Sea "Sutton-on-Sea"), famous for its golden sands, and the neighbouring village resort of [Trusthorpe](/wiki/Trusthorpe "Trusthorpe"). This area also offers leisure activities and has large caravan and holiday sites. The area is less developed, with fewer amusement arcades and nightclubs, and poorer road links to the rest of the county; but the area offers a more traditional seaside setting.
* The third group of resorts includes the seaside town of [Cleethorpes](/wiki/Cleethorpes "Cleethorpes") and the large village resort of [Humberston](/wiki/Humberston "Humberston") within North East Lincolnshire. It has the [Cleethorpes Coast Light Railway](/wiki/Cleethorpes_Coast_Light_Railway "Cleethorpes Coast Light Railway") and [Cleethorpes Pier](/wiki/Cleethorpes_Pier "Cleethorpes Pier"), along with golf courses and caravan and holiday sites, whilst it is also the former site of [Pleasure Island Family Theme Park](/wiki/Pleasure_Island_Family_Theme_Park "Pleasure Island Family Theme Park"). Cleethorpes is well served by road and rail; it is easily accessible from the [M180](/wiki/M180_motorway "M180 motorway") and the [TransPennine Express](/wiki/TransPennine_Express "TransPennine Express") route to Manchester.

Nature is an attraction for many tourists: the south-east of the county is mainly [fenland](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens") that attracts many species of birds, as do the [national nature reserves](/wiki/National_nature_reserve_(United_Kingdom) "National nature reserve (United Kingdom)") at [Gibraltar Point](/wiki/Gibraltar_Point,_Lincolnshire "Gibraltar Point, Lincolnshire"), [Saltfleetby-Theddlethorpe](/wiki/Saltfleetby-Theddlethorpe_Dunes_National_Nature_Reserve "Saltfleetby-Theddlethorpe Dunes National Nature Reserve") and [Donna Nook](/wiki/Donna_Nook "Donna Nook"), which also contains a large [grey seal](/wiki/Grey_seal "Grey seal") colony which is popular with visitors.

The market towns of the Lincolnshire Wolds, [Louth](/wiki/Louth,_Lincolnshire "Louth, Lincolnshire"), [Alford](/wiki/Alford,_Lincolnshire "Alford, Lincolnshire"), [Horncastle](/wiki/Horncastle,_Lincolnshire "Horncastle, Lincolnshire"), [Caistor](/wiki/Caistor "Caistor") and [Spilsby](/wiki/Spilsby "Spilsby") are also attractive, with several having historically important buildings, such as [Alford Manor House](/wiki/Alford_Manor_House "Alford Manor House"), [St James' Church, Louth](/wiki/St_James%27_Church,_Louth "St James' Church, Louth") and [Bolingbroke Castle](/wiki/Bolingbroke_Castle "Bolingbroke Castle"). The Wolds are popular for cycling and walking, with regular events such as the Lincolnshire Wolds Walking Festival.

The city of [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln,_England "Lincoln, England") is home to many tourist attractions including [Lincoln Castle](/wiki/Lincoln_Castle "Lincoln Castle"), [Lincoln Cathedral](/wiki/Lincoln_Cathedral "Lincoln Cathedral"), [The Engine Shed](/wiki/The_Engine_Shed "The Engine Shed"), [Steep Hill](/wiki/Steep_Hill "Steep Hill"), [International Bomber Command Centre](/wiki/International_Bomber_Command_Centre "International Bomber Command Centre") and [Guildhall and Stonebow](/wiki/Guildhall_and_Stonebow,_Lincoln "Guildhall and Stonebow, Lincoln") among other historical landmarks and listed buildings. The city is one of the many tourist centres in the East Midlands Region.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Steep_Hill.jpg/250px-Steep_Hill.jpg)](/wiki/File:Steep_Hill.jpg)

A view up 'Steep Hill' towards the historic quarter of Bailgate in Lincoln

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Mobile_Library_%28geograph_3691202%29.jpg/250px-Mobile_Library_%28geograph_3691202%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mobile_Library_(geograph_3691202).jpg)

Lincolnshire mobile library at [Pode Hole](/wiki/Pode_Hole "Pode Hole"). [Lincolnshire County Council](/wiki/Lincolnshire_County_Council "Lincolnshire County Council") operate five routes, covering small villages in this large, sparse, county. Each location is visited once a month.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Lincolnshire has a [rustic](/wiki/Rural_area "Rural area") culture. Due to the large distances between the towns, many villages have remained very self-contained, with many still having shops, pubs, local halls and local chapels and churches, offering a variety of social activities for residents. Fishing (in the extensive river and drainage system in the fens) and shooting are popular activities. A lot of the culture in Lincoln itself is based upon its history. [Lincoln Museum](/wiki/Lincoln_Museum,_Lincolnshire "Lincoln Museum, Lincolnshire") is an archaeological museum and art gallery in Lincoln. Lincoln Cathedral also plays a large part in Lincoln's culture, hosting many events throughout the year, from concert recitals to indoor food markets.

A Lincolnshire tradition was that front doors were used for only three things: a new baby, a bride, and a coffin.[[70]](#cite_note-Sayings_and_Traditions-70)

### People

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: People")]

See also: [List of people from Lincolnshire](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Lincolnshire "List of people from Lincolnshire")

Those born in Lincolnshire are sometimes given the nickname of [Yellowbellies](/wiki/Yellowbelly_(Lincolnshire) "Yellowbelly (Lincolnshire)") (often spelt "Yeller Bellies", to reflect the pronunciation of the phrase by the typical Lincolnshire farmer). The origin of this term is debated but is most commonly believed to derive from the uniform of the [10th Regiment of Foot](/wiki/10th_Regiment_of_Foot "10th Regiment of Foot") (later the [Lincolnshire Regiment](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Regiment "Lincolnshire Regiment")) which featured yellow facings. For this reason, the [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of Lincolnshire County Council is supported by two officers of the regiment.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### Local dialect

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Local dialect")]

In common with most other [Northern](/wiki/English_English#Northern_England "English English") and [Midlands dialects](/wiki/English_English#Midlands "English English") in England, "flat" *a* is preferred, i.e. [/bæθ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") over [/bɑːθ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), and also traditionally in words like 'water', pronounced /ˈwætər/ *watter* (though such a pronunciation is rarely heard nowadays). Similarly, /ʌ/ is usually replaced by /ʊ/. Features rather more confined to Lincolnshire include:

* Elaboration of [Received Pronunciation](/wiki/Received_Pronunciation "Received Pronunciation") English /eɪ/ or /iː/ into a complex [triphthong](/wiki/Triphthong "Triphthong") approximating, and often [transcribed](/wiki/Transcription_(linguistics) "Transcription (linguistics)") *-air-* or *-yair-*. For example: 'mate' [m(j)ɛːət]; 'beast' [b(j)ɛːəst]; *tates* (potatoes) [t(j)ɛːəts].
* An equivalent elaboration of standard English /oʊ/ – commonly [oː] in [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England") – into *-ooa-*. For example, 'boat' [bʊːət].
* Insertion of an extra [schwa](/wiki/Schwa "Schwa") into the standard English [diphthong](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong") /aʊ/.
* [Vocabulary](/wiki/Vocabulary "Vocabulary"): 'duck' as a term of endearment or informal address, 'mardy' meaning upset or angry, *mowt* (pronounced like 'mout') for 'might', *while* as a substitute for standard English 'until', *frit* meaning frightened, *grufty* meaning dirty or disgusting, and the inimitable [salutation](/wiki/Salutation_(greeting) "Salutation (greeting)") *now then!?* (hello), sometimes written *nairn* to reflect pronunciation.
* In the north-east of the county, around Grimsby and Immingham, the [nurse-square merger](/wiki/Phonological_history_of_English_vowels "Phonological history of English vowels") can be heard, as is also the case along the east coast of Yorkshire and also in Liverpool. Words that take /ɜː/ in RP take /ɛː/ in these areas.

Lincolnshire has its own dialect "champion", a farmer from the village of [Minting](/wiki/Minting "Minting") called Farmer Wink (real name Robert Carlton), who has produced videos about rural life, narrated in his broad Lincolnshire accent. A resident of [Woodhall Spa](/wiki/Woodhall_Spa "Woodhall Spa") has published a dictionary of words once prevalent in parts of the county.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Music")]

"[The Lincolnshire Poacher](/wiki/The_Lincolnshire_Poacher "The Lincolnshire Poacher")", a folksong, is the country's best-known melody and almost its unofficial anthem. It describes the delights of nocturnal poaching. It was the regimental quick march of the [10th Regiment of Foot](/wiki/10th_Regiment_of_Foot "10th Regiment of Foot") and its successors the [Royal Lincolnshire Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Lincolnshire_Regiment "Royal Lincolnshire Regiment") and the 2nd Battalion [Royal Anglian Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Anglian_Regiment "Royal Anglian Regiment"),[[75]](#cite_note-thelincolnshireregiment-75) who are known as "the Poachers".[[76]](#cite_note-armynavyairforce-76)

Lincolnshire was historically associated with the [Lincolnshire bagpipes](/wiki/Lincolnshire_bagpipes "Lincolnshire bagpipes"), instruments derided as coarse and unpleasant in contemporary literature, but noted as very popular in the county. The last player, [John Hunsley](/wiki/John_Hunsley "John Hunsley") of Middle Manton,[[77]](#cite_note-77) died in 1851,[[78]](#cite_note-78) and since then the instrument has been extinct.

The Australian composer [Percy Grainger](/wiki/Percy_Grainger "Percy Grainger") made what are thought to be the first recordings of British Folksongs between 1906 and 1908 in Lincolnshire using a wax [Phonograph Cylinder](/wiki/Phonograph_Cylinder "Phonograph Cylinder"). These are now housed in the [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library").[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) They included songs sung by [Joseph Taylor](/wiki/Joseph_Taylor_(folk_singer) "Joseph Taylor (folk singer)") of Saxby-All-Saints who became the first folk singer to be commercially recorded and whose rendition of [Brigg Fair](/wiki/Brigg_Fair "Brigg Fair") inspired classical works by Grainger and [Frederick Delius](/wiki/Frederick_Delius "Frederick Delius"). In 1937, Grainger wrote his *[Lincolnshire Posy](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Posy "Lincolnshire Posy")* for wind band. The piece is a compilation of folk songs ("bunch of wildflowers") collected by the composer in and around the county of Lincolnshire.[[81]](#cite_note-81) [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams") was a frequent guest at [Gunby Hall](/wiki/Gunby_Hall "Gunby Hall"). The manuscript of his collected folksong "Daffodils" is in their collection.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

Several composers have lived and worked in the county. [William Byrd](/wiki/William_Byrd "William Byrd") was organist and master of the choristers at [Lincoln Cathedral](/wiki/Lincoln_Cathedral "Lincoln Cathedral") from 1563 to 1572. [John Taverner](/wiki/John_Taverner "John Taverner") was listed as a lay clerk at [Tattershall](/wiki/Tattershall "Tattershall") Collegiate Church in 1525 and also sang at [St Botolph's Church, Boston](/wiki/St_Botolph%27s_Church,_Boston "St Botolph's Church, Boston") under whose famous "stump" he is presumed to be buried.[[83]](#cite_note-83) [Thomas Linley](/wiki/Thomas_Linley_the_younger "Thomas Linley the younger"), composer, a friend of [Mozart](/wiki/Mozart "Mozart"), drowned in the lake at [Grimsthorpe Castle](/wiki/Grimsthorpe_Castle "Grimsthorpe Castle") near Bourne in 1778.[[84]](#cite_note-84) [Nicholas Maw](/wiki/Nicholas_Maw "Nicholas Maw") was born in [Grantham](/wiki/Grantham "Grantham") and [Peter Seabourne](/wiki/Peter_Seabourne "Peter Seabourne") lives in [East Kirkby](/wiki/East_Kirkby "East Kirkby").

### Food

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Food")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Lincolnshire_sausages_and_onions.jpg/250px-Lincolnshire_sausages_and_onions.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincolnshire_sausages_and_onions.jpg)

[Lincolnshire sausages](/wiki/Lincolnshire_sausages "Lincolnshire sausages")

Lincolnshire has a number of local dishes:

* [Stuffed chine](/wiki/Stuffed_chine "Stuffed chine") – this is salted neck-chine of a pig taken from between the shoulder blades, salted for up to ten months and stuffed with [parsley](/wiki/Parsley "Parsley") (other ingredients are normally kept secret), and served cold.[[85]](#cite_note-Ports1937-85)
* [Haslet](/wiki/Haslet "Haslet") – a type of pork loaf, also flavoured with sage (pronounced HAYSS-let or AYSS-let in Lincolnshire but HAZ-let in many other parts of the country).[[85]](#cite_note-Ports1937-85)
* [Lincolnshire sausages](/wiki/Lincolnshire_sausage "Lincolnshire sausage") – most butchers in Lincolnshire have their own secret recipe for these and a competition is held each year to judge the best sausages in the county. Traditional Lincolnshire sausages are made entirely from minced pork, stale bread crumb ([rusk](/wiki/Rusk "Rusk") is used nowadays) pepper, sage and salt. The skins should be natural casings which are made from the intestines of either sheep or pig.
* [Pork pies](/wiki/Pork_pie "Pork pie") – the same pork butchers will take a pride in their unique recipe for pork pies.
* [Giblet](/wiki/Giblets "Giblets") pie.[[85]](#cite_note-Ports1937-85)
* [Mutton](/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton "Lamb and mutton") stuffed with [oysters](/wiki/Oyster "Oyster").[[85]](#cite_note-Ports1937-85)
* Plum bread – as with [plum pudding](/wiki/Christmas_pudding "Christmas pudding"), plum refers to [dried fruit](/wiki/Dried_fruit "Dried fruit"), namely currants, raisins and sultanas, sometimes soaked in tea.
* Grantham Gingerbread – a hard white ginger biscuit.
* [Lincolnshire Poacher cheese](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Poacher_cheese "Lincolnshire Poacher cheese") – a cheddar-style cheese produced in [Alford](/wiki/Alford,_Lincolnshire "Alford, Lincolnshire"). Lincolnshire Poacher has won numerous awards over the years including Supreme Champion at the 1996/7 British Cheese Awards and Best British Cheese at the World Cheese awards in 2001/2.
* [Batemans](/wiki/Batemans_Brewery "Batemans Brewery") ales – a beer brewed in [Wainfleet](/wiki/Wainfleet,_Lincolnshire "Wainfleet, Lincolnshire") and served in many pubs in the county and further afield.
* There are several small breweries.
* Grimsby is renowned for its fishing industry, and historically *Grimsby Fish* has carried a premium price. Since the decline of the fishing industry following entry to the European Economic Community in the 1970s this is no longer the case, with the majority of fish sold at the town's fish market being brought overland from other ports. However, *Grimsby Fish* is still a recognised *product*, one associated with a particular area that specialises in and has expertise in a particular trade (cf *Sheffield steel*). In 2009 [smoked fish](/wiki/Traditional_Grimsby_smoked_fish "Traditional Grimsby smoked fish") from the town was granted [Protected Geographical Indication](/wiki/Protected_Geographical_Indication "Protected Geographical Indication") by the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), reflecting the unique smoking methods used by certain local fish companies.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Craft chocolatiers can be found throughout[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) the county, such as Hansens[[90]](#cite_note-90) in [Folkingham](/wiki/Folkingham "Folkingham").[[91]](#cite_note-91) In 2013 Redstar Chocolate's "Duffy's Venezuela Ocumare Milk" won a gold medal as best bean-to-bar.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) The factory is in Cleethorpes.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Annual events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Annual events")]

Every year the [Lincolnshire Agricultural Society](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Agricultural_Society "Lincolnshire Agricultural Society"), founded in 1869, stages the [Lincolnshire Agricultural Show](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Agricultural_Show "Lincolnshire Agricultural Show").[[95]](#cite_note-95) It is held on the Wednesday and Thursday of the last whole week of June at its showground at [Grange de Lings](/wiki/Grange_de_Lings "Grange de Lings"), a few miles north of Lincoln on the [A15](/wiki/A15_road_(Great_Britain) "A15 road (Great Britain)"). The show was first held here in 1958. First held around the year 1884, it is one of the largest agricultural shows in the country, and is attended by around 100,000 people over its two days. The showground is in regular use throughout the year for a wide range of other events and functions.

Smaller local agricultural shows, such as the [Heckington](/wiki/Heckington "Heckington") Show[[96]](#cite_note-96) can still be found. [Corby Glen](/wiki/Corby_Glen "Corby Glen") sheep fair[[97]](#cite_note-97) has been held since 1238.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Red_Arrows_03.jpg/250px-Red_Arrows_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red_Arrows_03.jpg)

The [Red Arrows](/wiki/Red_Arrows "Red Arrows"), based at [RAF Waddington](/wiki/RAF_Waddington "RAF Waddington") near Lincoln[[98]](#cite_note-98) are a popular attraction at the Waddington Air Show.

Each year [RAF Waddington](/wiki/RAF_Waddington "RAF Waddington") is the home to the RAF International Waddington Air Show. The two-day event attracts around 150,000 people and usually takes place during the first weekend of July. Since its inception over 35 countries have participated, with aircraft from around the globe attending the Lincolnshire Base. Beginning 2017, the event will be held at nearby RAF Scampton.

On the Monday before Easter, an unusual auction takes place in [Bourne](/wiki/Bourne,_Lincolnshire "Bourne, Lincolnshire") to let the grazing rights of the Whitebread Meadow.[[99]](#cite_note-99) Bidding takes place while two boys race toward the Queen's Bridge in Eastgate, the end of which dash is equivalent to the falling of the gavel. The whole affair dates back to the 1742 will of William Clay.

The [Haxey Hood](/wiki/Haxey_Hood "Haxey Hood") village competition takes place every January, as it has for over 700 years.

Stamford's Mid-Lent fair sees showmen converge on the town the week after [Mothering Sunday](/wiki/Mothering_Sunday "Mothering Sunday"), with rides and sideshows filling Broad Street, the Sheepmarket and the Meadows for a week. Stalls selling Grantham gingerbread and nougat are a traditional feature. The following week sees them in Grantham, on the way north for the Summer. [Roger Tuby](http://www.rogertuby.co.uk/) brings a small funfair to [Bourne](/wiki/Bourne,_Lincolnshire "Bourne, Lincolnshire") and then to Spalding in Spring and returns in Autumn at the end of the season.

The villages of [Tetford](/wiki/Tetford "Tetford") and [Salmonby](/wiki/Salmonby "Salmonby") hold an annual Scarecrow Festival in May every year.

The [Belchford](/wiki/Belchford "Belchford") Downhill Challenge which is held every two years: soapbox racers race down the hill at up to 30 km/h. The turnout has been up to 1,000.

[Lincoln Christmas Market](/wiki/Lincoln_Christmas_Market "Lincoln Christmas Market"), was a street market held throughout the historic area of the city at the start of December, it was one of the largest [Christmas markets](/wiki/Christmas_market "Christmas market") in Europe, attracting over 250,000 people[[100]](#cite_note-100) over the four-day event. Around the same time, Christmas lights are turned on in Bourne, Sleaford, Skegness, and other towns.

Throughout the summer the [Stamford Shakespeare Company](/wiki/Stamford_Shakespeare_Company "Stamford Shakespeare Company")[[101]](#cite_note-101) presents the Bard's plays in the open-air theatre at [Tolethorpe Hall](/wiki/Tolethorpe_Hall "Tolethorpe Hall"), which is actually in [Rutland](/wiki/Rutland "Rutland").

The [Spalding](/wiki/Spalding,_Lincolnshire "Spalding, Lincolnshire") Flower Parade was held in late spring every year between 1959 and 2013. Colourful floats decorated with tulip heads competed for a cup.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

Lost Village Festival is an annual music and arts event held in a secluded woodland near Norton Disney. Founded in 2015, the festival features a diverse program of live music, DJ sets, immersive theatre, comedy, and interactive experiences. [[1]](https://www.visitlincolnshire.com/events/lost-village-festival/)

The Asylum Steampunk Festival, frequently referred to simply as the Steampunk Festival in Lincoln, is an annual convention and celebration of steampunk culture held in the historic Cathedral Quarter of Lincoln, England. Established in 2009, it is recognized as one of the largest steampunk gatherings in Europe. The festival usually takes place over the August Bank Holiday weekend, drawing enthusiasts from across the globe.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Lincoln_Gold_Postbox.jpg/250px-Lincoln_Gold_Postbox.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincoln_Gold_Postbox.jpg)

The gold Victorian-style Penfold post box in Lincoln painted in recognition of Paralympian [Sophie Wells](/wiki/Sophie_Wells "Sophie Wells") who won the gold medal in the team Equestrian event at the [2012 Paralympic Games](/wiki/2012_Paralympic_Games "2012 Paralympic Games") in London. It is the only [post box painted gold](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics_and_Paralympics_gold_post_boxes "2012 Summer Olympics and Paralympics gold post boxes") in the county.

The main sports played in the county are [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") and [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"). Lincolnshire does not have a high sporting profile, mainly due to the lack of facilities and high-profile football teams. Probably the most well-known sporting venues in Lincolnshire are [Cadwell Park](/wiki/Cadwell_Park "Cadwell Park") near Louth, where a round of the British Motorbike Championship is held on the last Monday of August every year and the racecourse at [Market Rasen](/wiki/Market_Rasen "Market Rasen")

* Two teams from Lincolnshire play in the [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League"): [Lincoln City](/wiki/Lincoln_City_F.C. "Lincoln City F.C.") play in [League One](/wiki/Football_League_One "Football League One") and [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town_F.C. "Grimsby Town F.C.") play in [League Two](/wiki/Football_League_Two "Football League Two"). In non-league football, [Boston United](/wiki/Boston_United_F.C. "Boston United F.C.") and [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.") play in the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(division) "National League (division)"), and [Gainsborough Trinity](/wiki/Gainsborough_Trinity_F.C. "Gainsborough Trinity F.C.") play in the [Northern Premier League](/wiki/Northern_Premier_League "Northern Premier League"). A meeting between any of these clubs is a [Lincolnshire derby](/wiki/Lincolnshire_derby "Lincolnshire derby"); the most prominent meeting, having happened across [four of the top five tiers of English football](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"), is Lincoln City vs Grimsby Town.
* In [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire_County_Cricket_Club "Lincolnshire County Cricket Club") are a minor county and play in the [Minor Counties Championship](/wiki/Minor_Counties_Championship "Minor Counties Championship").[[104]](#cite_note-104)
* In [hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") [Lindum Hockey Club](/wiki/Lindum_Hockey_Club "Lindum Hockey Club") play in the north of Lincoln.
* [Scunthorpe Rugby Club](/wiki/Scunthorpe_Rugby_Club "Scunthorpe Rugby Club") are the most notable [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") team from Lincolnshire, and will play in the fifth level of the English league system in the 2017–18 season. Other notable teams include Market Rasen and Louth RUFC, Lincoln RFC, and [Boston RFC](/wiki/Boston_RFC_(England) "Boston RFC (England)").
* Lincolnshire is home to one racecourse, at [Market Rasen](/wiki/Market_Rasen_Racecourse "Market Rasen Racecourse").
* [Cadwell Park](/wiki/Cadwell_Park "Cadwell Park") is the only motor-racing course in Lincolnshire. There is a speedway track in Scunthorpe, home of the [Scunthorpe Scorpions](/wiki/Scunthorpe_Scorpions "Scunthorpe Scorpions"), and stock-car racing at a stadium at Orby, near [Skegness](/wiki/Skegness "Skegness").
* Lincolnshire has an [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") club, the [Lincolnshire Bombers](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Bombers "Lincolnshire Bombers"), which has existed in its current guise since 2005.
* Lincolnshire is home to the UK [roller derby](/wiki/Roller_derby "Roller derby") team, the [Lincolnshire Bombers Roller Girls](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Bombers_Roller_Girls "Lincolnshire Bombers Roller Girls"), which is sponsored by [Motörhead](/wiki/Mot%C3%B6rhead "Motörhead").[[105]](#cite_note-105)
* Lincolnshire is home to Lincolnshire Lions Rugby League who run an Open Age Men's team, playing in the Midlands Merit League since 2022.

### Symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Symbols")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Lincolnshire_flag.svg/250px-Lincolnshire_flag.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Lincolnshire_flag.svg)

The flag of the historic county of Lincolnshire

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/The_Lincoln_Imp_-_geograph.org.uk_-_356041.jpg/250px-The_Lincoln_Imp_-_geograph.org.uk_-_356041.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Lincoln_Imp_-_geograph.org.uk_-_356041.jpg)

The [Lincoln Imp](/wiki/Lincoln_Imp "Lincoln Imp") high above the choir on the southern side of Lincoln Cathedral

The unofficial anthem of the county is the traditional folk song, "[The Lincolnshire Poacher](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Poacher_(folk_song) "Lincolnshire Poacher (folk song)")", which dates from around 1776. A version of the song was the theme for [BBC Radio Lincolnshire](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Lincolnshire "BBC Radio Lincolnshire") for many years.

According to a 2002 marketing campaign by the charity [Plantlife](/wiki/Plantlife "Plantlife"), the [county flower](/wiki/County_flowers_of_the_United_Kingdom "County flowers of the United Kingdom") of Lincolnshire is the [common dog-violet](/wiki/Common_dog-violet "Common dog-violet").

In August 2005, [BBC Radio Lincolnshire](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Lincolnshire "BBC Radio Lincolnshire") and *Lincolnshire Life* magazine launched a vote for a [flag of Lincolnshire](/wiki/Flag_of_Lincolnshire "Flag of Lincolnshire") to represent the county. Six competing designs were voted upon by locals and the winning submission was unveiled in October 2005.[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107) Lincoln has its own flag – St George's flag with a Fleur-de-Lys.

The [Lincoln Imp](/wiki/Lincoln_Imp "Lincoln Imp") has symbolised cathedral, city and county for many years.[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109) In 2006 it was replaced as the brand of Lincolnshire County Council by the stylised version seen on the header here [[2]](http://www.lincolnshire.gov.uk/) which has lost even the unique pose of the carving.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Media")]

### Press

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Press")]

The county is home to one daily newspaper, the *[Grimsby Telegraph](/wiki/Grimsby_Telegraph "Grimsby Telegraph")* which is published in the town and whose circulation area ostensibly covers North East Lincolnshire, although it reaches as far south as Louth and Alford and as west as [Brigg](/wiki/Brigg "Brigg").

There are two further weekly papers which used to be published daily until 2011; the *[Lincolnshire Echo](/wiki/Lincolnshire_Echo "Lincolnshire Echo")* is published weekly from Lincoln and covers the majority of the county reaching as far north as Louth, and the *[Scunthorpe Telegraph](/wiki/Scunthorpe_Telegraph "Scunthorpe Telegraph")* which covers northern Lincolnshire. All three are ultimately owned by [Reach plc](/wiki/Reach_plc "Reach plc").

There are also a number of weekly papers serving individual towns published in the county by [Iliffe Media](/wiki/Yattendon_Group#Iliffe_Media "Yattendon Group"). One of these, the *[Stamford Mercury](/wiki/Stamford_Mercury "Stamford Mercury")*, claims to be Britain's oldest newspaper, although it is now a typical local weekly and no longer covers stories from the whole East Midlands as the archived copies did.

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Television")]

With the exception of a small area to the south-west of the county,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Lincolnshire is served from the [Belmont transmitting station](/wiki/Belmont_transmitting_station "Belmont transmitting station"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] receiving [ITV Yorkshire](/wiki/ITV_Yorkshire "ITV Yorkshire") and [BBC Yorkshire and Lincolnshire](/wiki/BBC_Yorkshire_and_Lincolnshire "BBC Yorkshire and Lincolnshire") regions. The BBC covers the county in *[Look North](/wiki/BBC_Look_North_(East_Yorkshire_and_Lincolnshire) "BBC Look North (East Yorkshire and Lincolnshire)")* news from [Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull"), with input from studios in Lincoln and Grimsby. ITV provides coverage through *[Calendar](/wiki/Calendar_(News) "Calendar (News)")* news.

From 1959 to July 1974 [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)") programmes were provided by [Anglia Television](/wiki/Anglia_Television "Anglia Television"), based in [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich") with news offices in Grimsby.[[110]](#cite_note-110) Following a transmitter change, ITV services were provided by [Yorkshire Television](/wiki/Yorkshire_Television "Yorkshire Television"). This company kept open the offices in Grimsby and opened further facilities in Lincoln, although both of these closed in the mid-1990s.

South-west Lincolnshire receives [BBC East Midlands](/wiki/BBC_East_Midlands "BBC East Midlands") and [ITV Central](/wiki/ITV_Central "ITV Central") which are broadcast from the [Waltham transmitting station](/wiki/Waltham_transmitting_station "Waltham transmitting station"). Many villages just west of the [Lincoln Cliff](/wiki/Lincoln_Cliff "Lincoln Cliff") cannot get a signal from Belmont due to [shadowing](/wiki/Fading "Fading") and instead get their TV from [Emley Moor transmitting station](/wiki/Emley_Moor_transmitting_station "Emley Moor transmitting station") near Huddersfield.

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Radio")]

BBC local radio is provided by [BBC Radio Lincolnshire](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Lincolnshire "BBC Radio Lincolnshire") and, in the north of the county, [BBC Radio Humberside](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Humberside "BBC Radio Humberside"). Commercial local radio serving the county includes [Greatest Hits Radio Yorkshire](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio_Yorkshire "Greatest Hits Radio Yorkshire"), [Heart East](/wiki/Heart_East "Heart East"), [Hits Radio East Yorkshire & North Lincolnshire](/wiki/Hits_Radio_East_Yorkshire_%26_North_Lincolnshire "Hits Radio East Yorkshire & North Lincolnshire"), [Hits Radio Lincolnshire](/wiki/Hits_Radio_Lincolnshire "Hits Radio Lincolnshire"), [Capital Yorkshire](/wiki/Capital_Yorkshire "Capital Yorkshire") (in the north of the county) and [Smooth East Midlands](/wiki/Smooth_East_Midlands "Smooth East Midlands"). The [community radio](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") stations in the county are [Endeavour FM](/wiki/Endeavour_FM "Endeavour FM") in Boston; [TMCR 95.3](/wiki/TMCR_95.3 "TMCR 95.3") covering [Epworth](/wiki/Epworth,_Lincolnshire "Epworth, Lincolnshire") and [Isle of Axholme](/wiki/Isle_of_Axholme "Isle of Axholme"); and LCR 103.6 in Lincoln.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

## Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Military")]

Typhoon FGR4 aircraft, based at [RAF Coningsby](/wiki/RAF_Coningsby "RAF Coningsby")

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Air")]

Main article: [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force")

Because of its flat geography and low population density, Lincolnshire is an ideal place for airfields, and the [Air Ministry](/wiki/Air_Ministry "Air Ministry") built prolifically with the county hosting nearly seventy separate air bases. It became known as "bomber county".[[112]](#cite_note-112) Since the end of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") most of these airfields or stations were decommissioned, but the RAF retains a significant footprint in Lincolnshire for the air defence of the United Kingdom and aircrew training. For more information on former bases, see [List of former RAF stations](/wiki/List_of_former_RAF_stations "List of former RAF stations").

Two major front-line bases located in Lincolnshire are [RAF Coningsby](/wiki/RAF_Coningsby "RAF Coningsby"), which is one of only two RAF [Quick Reaction Alert](/wiki/Quick_Reaction_Alert "Quick Reaction Alert") (QRA) Stations in the United Kingdom and home to the [Eurofighter Typhoon](/wiki/Eurofighter_Typhoon "Eurofighter Typhoon") jet fighters, and [RAF Waddington](/wiki/RAF_Waddington "RAF Waddington"), where most of the RAF's Intelligence, Surveillance, Target Acquisition and Reconnaissance aircraft are based. The [Red Arrows](/wiki/Red_Arrows "Red Arrows") Aerobatic Team has also been based at Waddington since October 2022 after their previous base, [RAF Scampton](/wiki/RAF_Scampton "RAF Scampton"), was closed down.[[113]](#cite_note-113) Other stations in Lincolnshire include [RAF Cranwell](/wiki/RAF_Cranwell "RAF Cranwell"), home to all Air Force Basic Officer Training for the Royal Air Force; [RAF Barkston Heath](/wiki/RAF_Barkston_Heath "RAF Barkston Heath"), a training airfield; and minor bases such as [RAF Donna Nook](/wiki/RAF_Donna_Nook "RAF Donna Nook") and [RAF Digby](/wiki/RAF_Digby "RAF Digby").

Lincolnshire is also home to two active RAF and NATO-allied air weapons training [bombing ranges](/wiki/Bombing_range "Bombing range"), located along [The Wash](/wiki/The_Wash "The Wash") and north Lincolnshire coastline—[RAF Holbeach](/wiki/RAF_Holbeach "RAF Holbeach") active since 1926 (originally part of the former [RAF Sutton Bridge](/wiki/RAF_Sutton_Bridge "RAF Sutton Bridge") station) and [Donna Nook](/wiki/RAF_Donna_Nook "RAF Donna Nook"). The [RAF Wainfleet](/wiki/RAF_Wainfleet "RAF Wainfleet") range was decommissioned in 2010.

### Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lincolnshire&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Army")]

The Army runs [Sobraon Barracks](/wiki/Sobraon_Barracks "Sobraon Barracks"), home of 160 (Lincoln) Squadron, [Royal Logistic Corps](/wiki/Royal_Logistic_Corps "Royal Logistic Corps") (RLC), as well as [Prince William of Gloucester Barracks](/wiki/Prince_William_of_Gloucester_Barracks "Prince William of Gloucester Barracks"), Grantham, home to the national specialist logistics units. In November 2016 the [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)") announced that the Grantham site would close in 2020[[114]](#cite_note-114) but the timescale has twice been extended, latterly to 2028.[[115]](#cite_note-115)
